message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We aimed to harness IDentif.AI 2.0, a clinically actionable AI platform to rapidly pinpoint and prioritize optimal combination therapy regimens against COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A pool of starting candidate therapies was developed in collaboration with a community of infectious disease clinicians and included EIDD-1931 (metabolite of EIDD-2801), baricitinib, ebselen, selinexor, masitinib, nafamostat mesylate, telaprevir (VX-950), SN-38 (metabolite of irinotecan), imatinib mesylate, remdesivir, lopinavir, and ritonavir. Following the initial drug pool assessment, a focused, 6-drug pool was interrogated at 3 dosing levels per drug representing nearly 10,000 possible combination regimens. IDentif.AI 2.0 paired prospective, experimental validation of multi-drug efficacy on a SARS-CoV-2 live virus (propagated, original strain, B.1.351 and B.1.617.2 variants) and Vero E6 assay with a quadratic optimization workflow.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Within 3 weeks, IDentif.AI 2.0 realized a list of combination regimens, ranked by efficacy, for clinical go/no-go regimen recommendations. IDentif.AI 2.0 revealed EIDD-1931 to be a strong candidate upon which multiple drug combinations can be derived.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>IDentif.AI 2.0 rapidly revealed promising drug combinations for clinical translation. It pinpointed dose-dependent drug synergy behavior to play a role in trial design and realizing positive treatment outcomes. IDentif.AI 2.0 represents an actionable path towards rapidly optimizing combination therapy following pandemic emergence.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Graphical Abstract</jats:title>
                  <jats:fig id="ufig1" position="float" fig-type="figure" orientation="portrait">
                    <jats:graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="21259321v2_ufig1" position="float" orientation="portrait"/>
                  </jats:fig>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Highlights</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="simple">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>-</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>When novel pathogens emerge, the immediate strategy is to repurpose drugs.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>-</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>Good drugs delivered together in suboptimal combinations and doses can yield low or no efficacy, leading to misperception that the drugs are ineffective.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>-</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>IDentif.AI 2.0 does not use in silico modeling or pre-existing data.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>-</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>IDentif.AI 2.0 pairs optimization with prospectively acquired experimental data using a SARS-CoV-2/Vero E6 assay.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>-</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>IDentif.AI 2.0 pinpoints EIDD-1931 as a foundation for optimized anti-SARS-CoV-2 combination therapies.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: The IDentif.AI 2.0 Pandemic Readiness Platform: Rapid Prioritization of Optimized COVID-19 Combination Therapy Regimens
